ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Burmese glass noodle salad (thoke). FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP (noodle-based dishes) used as closest parent; no specific code for Burmese mala glass noodle salad exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Burmese glass noodle salad.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Burmese mala chicken glass noodle salad is approximately 300 g, typically served as a single-portion plate.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: glass noodles (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168870), cooked chicken breast (USDA FDC ID 171477), mala seasoning and salad dressing components (USDA SR Legacy analogues); cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Southeast Asian noodle salad dishes.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central / FAO INFOODS SEA
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for ကြက်မာလာချည်ငံစပ်သုတ် (Burmese mala chicken glass noodle salad) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values estimated from weighted component analysis: approximately 40% glass noodles (cooked), 25% cooked chicken breast, 15% mala/chilli dressing (oil, spices), 10% vegetables (shallots, garlic, coriander), 10% peanuts and sesame. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used for individual ingredient values. Composite calculated per 100 g of finished dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of glass noodles (mung bean vermicelli) and mixed noodle salad analogues; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese mala chicken glass noodle salad. GI estimated at 45 based on glass noodles (mung bean vermicelli) GI of approximately 39-45 (low GI) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The addition of protein (chicken), fat (sesame oil, peanuts), acid (lime juice), and fibre from vegetables further lowers the effective GI of the composite dish. GL per serving calculated as (45 × 16.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 300 g serving) / 100 = 22.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and mala seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce and mala paste, as a 300 g serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from glass noodles, partially offset by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of glass noodles (GI ~45) and the presence of protein and fat in this dish moderate the postprandial glucose response, but the glycemic load per serving (~22 g) is moderate, so individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with non-starchy vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from mala spice oil and peanuts
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1260 mg per serving) and moderate fat from mala oil and peanuts are relevant for cardiovascular disease management; individuals should limit frequency of consumption and choose preparations with reduced oil and fish sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: ကြက်မာလာချည်ငံစပ်သုတ် (Kyat Ma La Chay Ngan Sat Thoke) is a Burmese spicy glass noodle salad featuring cooked chicken tossed with mala (numbing-spicy) seasoning, glass noodles (bean thread or mung bean vermicelli), chilli, garlic, shallots, fish sauce, lime juice, sesame oil, roasted peanuts, and fresh coriander. The dish belongs to the Burmese 'thoke' (salad) tradition, which encompasses a wide variety of tossed salads made with noodles, vegetables, or starches dressed with pungent, sour, and spicy condiments. The mala element introduces Sichuan-influenced numbing heat from Sichuan peppercorns and dried chilli, reflecting the culinary exchange between Myanmar and Yunnan, China. The texture is a contrast of slippery glass noodles, tender chicken, and crunchy peanuts, with a bold flavour profile that is simultaneously spicy, sour, savoury, and aromatic. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~148 kcal per 100 g), a useful protein contribution from chicken (~9.2 g per 100 g), and a low glycemic index (~45) due to the glass noodle base and the moderating effect of protein and fat. However, sodium content is high (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1260 mg per 300 g serving) due to fish sauce and mala seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and typically halal when prepared with halal chicken and fish sauce. It is served as a main course or shared dish in Burmese restaurants in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for this specific dish exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for individual ingredients. GI is estimated by analogy from glass noodle GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this estimation and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in the component ingredients per USDA SR Legacy data. All other -1 values in per_100g are populated with estimated values from component analysis. The food_group 'Grains and staples' is preserved from the input record as the primary energy source is glass noodles. The cuisine_tags 'Lor Mee' from the input are likely a field survey platform tagging artefact and are not used in this record, as this dish is a Burmese thoke (salad), not a Singaporean lor mee. Mala seasoning reflects Sichuan-Burmese culinary influence. Sodium content is notably high and is the primary clinical concern for this dish.
